California
Fair POlitical
Practices Comrnission
May 17, L99O
Peter Bentley
carpinterians Against Ranpant Development,
A save our city Committee
Box 91L
Carpinteria, cA 930L3
Re: Your Request for Advice
our f ile No. A-90-265
Dear Mr. Bentley:
You have requested advice on behalf of Carpinterians Against
Rampant Development (CARD) and Save our city (Soc) concerning the
campaign provisions of the Political Reforn Act (the rrActtr). r' on
April, 20, 1990, and lttay 2 , l-990, I spoke with you and received
sone additional i-nformation.
FACTS
During our telephone conversations, you stated that save our
city is the organization referred to in your l-etter and CARD is
the conmittee, that you are one of four directors for soc, and
that you are authorized to request advice on behalf of Soc as weII
as CARD, You also said that SOC was formed about a year ago to
educate residents of carpinteria and the surrounding area about
issues related to the quality of life, that CARD was forrned to
oppose a Local ballot measure, and that CARD does not make
contributions to candidates. In addition, you stated that SOC
receives j.ts funds from donations, but it has never made political
contributions and has not solicited or received any contributions
to support or oppose candidates or ballot measures. SOC is now
considering making contributions to CARD.
OUESTIONS
1. May SoC rnake unlimited contributions to CARD without
disclosing the sources of those funds?
1 
Go.r".r,nent code Sections 81ooo-9101.5. Al-I statutory
references are to the Government code unress otherwise i-ndicatea.
commission regulations appear at 2 california code of Regurations
section 18000, et seq. Arr references to regurations ar6 to titre
2, Division 6 of the CaLifornia Code of Regulations.
'1JB J Strect' Suite *L)o o p.o. B.x trlrz o sacranrent- cA 95tttr4-oti(r7 a (9r6) ]2r-i66tl


Bentl,ey, Peter
Page 2
2. May SOc make contributions to candidates, su6ject to
applicable contribution Iimits, without disclosure of the sources
of those funds?
Uay CARD or SOC receive non-monetary contributions?
CONCLUSIONS
1. Assuming that SoC has not received and does not receive
contributions totalling 
$l-,000 or more in a calendar year for
political 
purposes, it may make unlimited contributions to CARD
during 1990 and vrill not be required to disclose contributions it
receives. HovJever, if SOC makes contributions totalling 
$10,000
or more in a calendar year to candidates or committees (including
CARD), it will qualify as a trmajor donor'r committee and must file
reports disclosing the contributions that it makes. If in any
subsequent year SOC nakes contributions to California state or
Iocal candidates or committees totalling 
$1,00O or nore, it wiII
qualify as a rrrecipientt committee and must file reports
disclosing contributions it receives as lreLl, as contributions that
it nakes.
2. Assuming that SOC has not received and does not receive
contributions totall-ing $1,000 or more in a calendar year for
political 
purposes, it nay nake contributions to candidates during
1990 and will not be required to di-sclose the contributions it
receives. Hovrever, if SOC makes contributions totalling 
$1O,OOO
or more in a calendar year to candidates or committees (including
CARD), it will qual-ify as a rrmajor donorrr committee and nust fite
reports disclosing the contributions that it makes. If in any
subsequent year SOC makes contributions to California state or
local candidates or cornrnittees totalling 
91,OOO or more, it will
qualify as a lrrecipientrr committee and must file reports
disclosing contributions it receives as weII as contributions that
it rnakes .
3. 
CARD or SOC nay receive non-monetary contributions.
Hovrever, if SOc receives monetary or non-monetary contributions
for political 
purposes aggregating $1,OOO or more during a
calendar year, it will quarify as a rrrecipientrr comrnitt6e and must
file reports disclosing contributions reclived and expenditures
rnade .
3.
ANALYS I S
. 
1..and 2. proposition 73 imposed
to candidates and to committees which
I irnits on contributions made
make contributions to


Bentley, Peter
Page 3
candidates.2 lsections 853oL-85303.) The Act does not limit
contributions to comrnittees which are formed for purposes other
than naking contributions to candidates, such as committees formed
to support or oppose ballot measures.
Section 82015 def i,nes rrcontributionrr as:
...a payment, a forgiveness of a 1oan, a paynent of a loan by
a third party, or an enforceable pronise to make a payment
except to tfre extent that fuII and adequate consideration is
recelved unless it is clear from the surrounding
circunstances that it is not nade for political 
purposes.
An expenditure made at the behest of a candidate, co:nmittee
or elLcted officer is a contribution to the candi'date,
commj.ttee or elected officer unless fuII and adequate
consideration is received for naking the expenditure'
Regutation 18215 (copy enclosed) clarifies that a payrnent is
made for "political 
purposesrt if it is:
(1) For the purpose of inf l-uencing or attenptj-ng
to iniluence the action of the voters for or against the
nomination or election of a candidate or candidates, or
the qualification or passage of any measure; or
(2) Received by or made at the behest of:
2 
Th. Act lirnits contributions which a candj.date rnay receive
fron a rrpersontr to $1,000 per f iscal- year (July l- - June 30). 
A
trpersonrt includes an individuat, business entity, or other entity
which does not receive contributions for political- purposes.
Contributions from a t'potitical committeerr to a candidate are
limited to $2,500 per f j,scal yearr and contributions from a
political 
party or a rrbroad based political 
conmitteerr to a
candidate are limited to $5,000 per fiscal year. contributions to
ttpolitical committees" and rrbroad based political 
comrnitteesrr are
linited to $2,500 per fiscal year from any source. A "political
committeerr is a group of persons that receives contributions from
two or more persons and makes contributions to candidates,
(Sections 85L02(c).) A 'rbroad based political, comrnitteerr is a
comrnittee of persons which has been in exj.stence for more than six
months, receives contributions from one hundred or more persons,
and acting in concert makes contributions to five or more
candidates. (Section 85102 (d) . ) Enclosed is an j-nformation
manuaL which summarizes these provisions.


Bentl,ey, Peter
Page 4
(A) A candidate, unless it is clear from
surrounding circumstances that the payment t as
received or made at his or her behest for personal
purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy or
status as an office holder. The term rrpaymentrr
includes the candidate's o$/n money or property used
on behalf of his or her candidacy;
(B) A controlled committee;
(C) An official 
committee of a political
party, including a state central conrnittee, county
central comnittee, assembly district 
comnittee or
any subcommittee of such committee; or
(D) An organization formed or existing
primarily for political 
purposes as defined in
subsection (a) (1), including but not lirnited to a
political 
action committee establ-ished by any
menbership organization, labor union or
corporation .
(b) 
rrMade at the behestrr means a payrnent made
under the control or at the direction of, in
cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert
with, or at the request or suggestion of a candidate,
controlled committee, official 
committee of a political
party, or organization formed or existing prirnarily for
political 
purposes.
Section 82013 defines three types of I'committeestr which must
file periodic canpaign dj.sclosure reports.
tlCommitteerr means any person or combination of persons
who directly or indirectly does any of the following:
(a) Receives contributions totaling one thousand
dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar year;
(b) Makes independent expenditures totating one
thousand dollars ($1,000) or nore in a calendar yeari or
(c) Makes contributions totaLing ten thousand dollars
($10,000) or more in a calendar year to or at the behest of
candidates or committees....
You have indicated that although SoC is funded by donations,
it has not solicited or received contributions specif-icalIy for
the purpose of making contributions or expenditures to support or
oppose candidates or.balLot measures, and has not to date- i:sed any
of the funds it receives for that purpose. Therefore, SOC has nol
qualified as a "recipientr committee pursuant to Section 82013(a).


Bentley, Peter
Page 5
Hohrever, SOC will qualify as a recipient committee if it
solicits or receives contributions in the future for the purpose
of supporting or opposing candidates or ballot measures. In
addition, non-political donations received by SOC will rneet the
Act's definition of ttcontribution[ and SOC \"ri1]- qualify as a
recipient comnittee if SOC uses those donations for political
purposes in more than one calendar year. ReguLation 1821-5
provides :
(c) "Contribution" includes any monetary or non-
monetary payment for which fuII and adequate consider-
ation is not made to the donor that is received by any
person or organization other than a candidate,
controlled cornrnittee, official 
committee of a political
party, or organization formed or existing primarily for
political 
purposes if the payment is rtearnarked'r for the
naking of contribution or expenditures. A payment is
rrearmarked'r r,rhen, at the time of naking the payment, the
donor knows or has reason to know that the paynent or
funds with which the payment will be comrningled wiLl be
used to make contributions, as defined in Government
code Section 820L5 and this regulation, or expenditures,
as def i,ned in Governrnent code Section 82025 and 2 cal.
Adn. code Section 1a225. If the donor knows or has
reason to know that only part of the paynent will be
used to make contributions or expenditures, the payment
sha1I be apportioned on a reasonable basis in order to
determine the anount of contribution. 
Factors relevant
to a determination that the donor has reason to know
that al1 or part of the paynent will be used to make
expenditures and contributions include but are not
linited to the estabLished practice of the person or
organization with respect to expenditures and
contributions and any representations made when the
Payment is solicited.
The Conmission has advised that an organization which uses
membership dues or other non-pol-itical donations to support or
oppose candidates or ballot measures, and which has not notified
its members that it will use the funds to make contributions or
expenditures, will qualify as a recipient comnittee under Section
82o13(a) during the second year in which it engages in such
activities. 
That is because any contributions and expenditures
made during the first calendar year in which the organization
engages in such activities establish d historyrr that puts the
organization,s donors on notice that sorne or all of their funds
may be used for political purposes. If the organization makes
contributions or other expenditures for politi-aI 
purposes
totalling 
$1,000 or more during any subsequent calendir year, it
will be deerned to have ,,received,, contriUutions totall-in! 
gt,OOO
or more. (Enclosed is a fact sheet which provides further
information regarding this provision. )


Bentley, Peter
Page 6
Although it appears frorn the facts you have provided that soc
wil-I not qualify as a recipient cornmittee during 1990, SOC may
still 
incur campaign disclosure obligations this year. If soc
makes contributions to or at the behest of candidates or
committees totalling 
$10,000 or more in the calendar year it witl
qualify as a "major donor[ comnittee under Section 820]-3 (c) .
section 84200(b) requires that major donor comrnittees file
campaign statements no later than JuIy 31, 1990 for the period
covering January l- through June 30, and January 31, 1991- for the
period covering JuIy 1 through December 31. If SOC nakes
independent expenditures totatling $1,000 or more in the calendar
year, it will qualify as an rrindependent expendj.turert committee
under Section 820L3(b), and nust file disclosure reports under
section 8420o (b) .
3. Nothing in the Act or reguldtions prohibits the rnaking of
non-monetary (in-kind) contributions. 
If there is some
understanding at the tirne a non-monetary contribution is made or
received that the donor may be repai-d, the contribution rnust be
disclosed as an "in-kind loan. " 
(Refer to the enclosed
rrlnforrnation Manual on Campaign Disclosure Provisj-ons of the
PoLitical Reform Act'r for information on reporting in-kind 1oans. )
Non-monetary contributions made to Soc count toward the $1,000
threshold for qualifying as a recipient committee.
We also discussed the possibility that CARD may be
rf sponsored" by Soc. section a2o4a.7 provides:
(a) rrsponsored committee'r means a cornmittee other than a
candidate controlled committee, which has one or more
sponsors. Any person, except a candidate or other
individual-, may sponsor a comrnittee.
(b) A person sponsors a comrnittee if any of the following
apply:
(1) The committee receives all or nearly all of its
contributions frorn the person or its members, officers,
enployees, or shareholders.
(21 The person colLects contributions for the conmittee
by use of payroll deductions or dues from its nenbers,
officers, or employees.
(3) The person alone or in cornbination with other
organizations, provides aII or nearly atl of the
adninistrative services for the committee.


Bentley, Peter
Page 7
(4) The person, alone or in cornbination srith other
organizations, sets the polj,cies for soLiciting contributions
or making expenditures of committee funds.
you stated that although CARD's stationery indicates that it
is a committee of SOC, SOC does not meet any of the criterj-a
established in Section 82048.7 and does not sponsor CARD.
If you have any further questions concerning this natter
please contact ne at (916) 322-5662.
S incere ly
Kathryn E. Donovan
General counsel
